item.title: Subnautica 2 Preview - Diving Deeper
item.content:encoded:
Far into the future, a group of colonists boards the Alterra spaceship CICADA in the hopes of finding a new home on an alien world. Unfortunately, disaster strikes, and the CICADA is destroyed. In our Subnautica 2 preview, I'll explore the latest entry in the franchise from Unknown Worlds Entertainment.

Subnautica 2 once again drops you into the depths of an alien ocean world with few supplies and a large area to explore. It looks similar to Subnautica and Subnautica: Below Zero at first glance, but don't be fooled — everything you loved about the first two games has been greatly improved.

Into the Grand Blue

Subnautica 2 diverges somewhat from the previous games with a playable tutorial. Aside from familiarizing new players with the game, this tutorial also helps introduce new concepts to veterans of the franchise, such as the "Adaptations" system: permanent genetic buffs that upgrade your character.

The shallow starting area feels much like the opening hours of Subnautica, but you'll quickly learn that this sequel has many new surprises in store.

As with its predecessor, Subnautica 2 then drops you into the actual game world in an escape pod with a few days of food and little else. You'll have to free dive into the ocean to collect crafting materials and create tools to help you set up a base camp to explore the world.

The starting area feels safe and shallow; there is abundant food and water available, and plenty of crafting materials to help you put your base together. The shallow starting area feels much like the opening hours of Subnautica, but you'll quickly learn that this sequel has many new surprises in store.

Don't stray too far from home, though — the map borders are patrolled by the world's scariest border patrol in the form of an undetectable, unavoidable creature that one-shots you. You thankfully get warned that you're headed out of bounds, but it's easy to lose loot or a Tadpole submarine if you're not careful.

Making Your Habitat a Home

One of the first big changes you'll notice is a greatly enhanced building system. Previously, Subnautica gave you prefabricated modules of a defined size to serve as the building blocks of your base. Base pieces still start out at a standard defined size, but they don't have to stay that way.

Most of your underwater base's structure can be grown or shrunk at varying scales, assuming you have the resources. This makes it easy to get a room to just the right size for your needs — no more worries about whether or not you can fit all of your machinery into a particular base module.

Power generation has improved, too. The new Hydroelectric Turbine is pricey at the cost of 3 Titanium, 3 Copper, and 3 Silver, but it generates a steady 12 units of 12 energy per second as long as it's in a natural underwater current. You can, of course, opt to use Solar Panels, Thermal Generators, or the Bioreactor, too.

Of course, this new building system has its limitations, too. You can only grow or shrink buildings horizontally; building vertically makes new floors of a defined vertical height. Slow and cumbersome ladders are your only option for moving between levels at this time, making multi-story bases unpalatable.

Fighting Fauna

Subnautica 2 carries on the tradition of a distinct lack of lethal weaponry. That's not to say you won't have any way to defend yourself, though.

At first, your only option for self-defense is running away or using Distraction Flares to draw the attention of larger, hostile underwater creatures. Many of these creatures are much faster than you underwater, and fleeing without getting injured or killed can be a challenge.

It beggars belief that interstellar colonists would head to an alien planet without the means to defend themselves from hostile wildlife.

Eventually, you'll unlock the Sonic Resonator. Though this tool is intended for breaking up large formations of crafting materials, it can also be used to stun an enemy and push it away in a similar fashion to Lúcio's Soundwave alt-fire in Overwatch 2. You can later upgrade the Sonic Resonator to have a ranged projectile.

The lack of lethal weaponry remains one of the more contentious points of the Subnautica franchise, and I still don't like it. It beggars belief that interstellar colonists would head to an alien planet without the means to defend themselves from hostile wildlife; it is one of the sillier aspects of the lore.

Still, you can protect yourself if you need to, albeit with inadequate weaponry. If you're hoping to take out alien sharks with space harpoons, though, you'll be sorely disappointed.

Alien Planet

It's well-known that Subnautica 2 takes place on an alien planet, so you will, of course, encounter lots of interesting otherworldly creatures. These are far from the only oddities you'll see in this new world. (If you wish to avoid spoilers, it's best to skip ahead to the next section.)

The first Subnautica game had a dead alien civilization and an extraterrestrial virus as major plot and gameplay elements, and Subnautica 2 is no different. In fact, it seems that there may have been more than one alien civilization on the planet.

As you explore the planet, you'll come across audio logs from colonists talking about encountering other creatures. It's not quite as explicit as you like, but it seems that there may be another sapient species somewhere in the world that you have yet to encounter.

The interplay of these audio logs and alien ruins adds a lot of flavor to the world. The first Early Access build culminates with you bringing some massive alien machinery online, and it certainly leaves you with a feeling of excitement for the future of this game's story.

Subnautica 2 Preview | Final Thoughts

I played Subnautica 2 for 17 hours, completing all of the content available in the first Early Access build. While I didn't explore every single nook and cranny, the small portion of the world that exists right now feels dense, detailed, and full of life.

As always, Subnautica 2 is an Early Access game, and everything is subject to change. That said, Unknown Worlds Entertainment has a strong track record of finishing its projects, and I expect to see Subnautica 2 grow into something as good as (if not better than) its predecessor.

If you're fond of survival crafting games and exploration, you'll be happy to hear that you can expect a solid 15+ hours of gameplay with the content currently in the game. If you'd prefer to wait for the game to be finished, the Early Access period is expected to take roughly 2 years.

I thoroughly enjoyed my time with Subnautica 2; it felt familiar to Subnautica in all of the right ways while also improving damn near everything in the game. Whether you're new to the franchise or a salty old Subnautica veteran, Subnautica 2 is absolutely worth your time.

Subnautica 2 was previewed on PC via Steam with a code provided by the publisher over approximately 17 hours of gameplay. All images were taken during the preview process.

One of my earliest memories is playing Super Mario Bros. on the Nintendo Entertainment System. I've had a controller in my hand since I was 4… More about Robert N



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/subnautica-2-preview
